title: How the Hagia Sophia Was Built, and How ItБ─≥s Being Saved from Collapse
description:
in Architecture, History |   	June 11th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Ask around for what everyбґone knows about Istanбґbul (othбґer than that it us
body:
in Architecture, History |   	June 11th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Ask around for what everyбґone knows about Istanбґbul (othбґer than that it used to be called Conбґstanбґtinoбґple), and youБ─≥ll find that the presбґence of Hagia Sophia there comes right to many a mind. Less likeбґly to be menбґtioned is its proneбґness to earthбґquakes, though it tends to rank just below Tokyo on lists of cities under the greatбґest threat from fault lines below. These two charбґacбґterбґisбґtics turn out to have a conбґnecбґtion, manбґiбґfest in the ongoбґing seisбґmic retroбґfitting of IstanбґbulБ─≥s symбґbolбґic catheбґdral-turned-mosque-turned-museбґum turned-mosque-again. Hagia Sophia is one of the most celбґeбґbratбґed reliбґgious buildбґings standбґing; keepбґing it that way requires a seriбґous engiбґneerбґing effort, as explained in the new B1M video above.
Since it was first built in the fourth cenбґtuбґry, Hagia Sophia has actuбґalбґly susбґtained severe earthбґquake damбґage quite a few times, includбґing a comбґplete colбґlapse of its cupoбґla in the year 558 and parбґtial colбґlapsбґes in the tenth and fourбґteenth cenбґturies. The conбґstrucбґtion of its famous cenбґtral dome, along with the smallбґer sub-domes that supбґport it, gets a secбґtion of its own in the video.
Hostб═Fred Mills also gives due menбґtion to the eight green marбґble columns that supбґport the upper floors of the catheбґdral, thought to have been recyбґcled from the ruins of the Temбґple of Artemis (one of the Sevбґen Wonбґders of the Ancient World), and the red stone set into the floor on which emperбґors were once crowned that would have been brought in from the Egyptбґian desert.
In these and othбґer respects, Hagia Sophia isnБ─≥t just a site of pilбґgrimбґage and worбґship, but also a verбґiбґtaбґble built record of cenбґturies upon cenбґturies of Roman, Greek, Chrisбґtбґian, and Islamбґic civбґiбґlizaбґtion. As eviбґdenced by the scafбґfoldбґing curбґrentбґly up to facilбґiбґtate the project of readyбґing it for the inevitable comбґing of the big one Б─■ or rather, the bigбґger one Б─■ the strucбґture conбґtinбґues to change with time, though our era has an espeбґcialбґly strong conбґcern for preбґservбґing what have by now become hisбґtorбґiбґcal feaбґtures. Hence the efforts now being put into restoraбґtion: of the dome, natбґuбґralбґly, but also of the floors, columns, and mosaics. If all goes well, Hagia Sophia will conбґtinбґue to stand as the most strikбґing strucбґture in IstanбґbulБ─≥s already draбґmatбґic urban and geoбґgraphбґiбґcal setбґting for anothбґer milбґlenбґniбґum and a half, incorбґpoбґratбґing hisбґtoбґry all the while.
Relatбґed conбґtent:
An Introбґducбґtion to Hagia Sophia: After 85 Years as a Museбґum, ItБ─≥s Set to Become a Mosque Again
How the Byzanбґtine Empire Rose, Fell, and Creбґatбґed the Gloбґriбґous Hagia Sophia: A Hisбґtoбґry in Ten Aniбґmatбґed Minбґutes
A Culбґturбґal Tour of Istanбґbul, Where the Art and Hisбґtoбґry of Three Great Empires Come Togethбґer
360 Degree Virбґtuбґal Tours of the Hagia Sophia
Hear the Hagia SophiaБ─≥s Awe-Inspirбґing Acoustics Get Recreбґatбґed with Comбґputбґer Simбґuбґlaбґtions, and Let Yourбґself Get Transбґportбґed Back to the Midбґdle Ages
Istanбґbul Capбґtured in Beauбґtiбґful Colбґor Images from 1890: The Hagia Sophia, Topбґkaбґki PalaceБ─≥s Impeбґrбґiбґal Gate & More
Based in Seoul,б═Colбґinб═Marshallб═writes and broadбґcasts on cities, lanбґguage, and culбґture. HeБ─≥s the author of the newsletбґterб═Books on Citiesб═as well as the booksб═М∙°Й╣ґ Л ■Л∙Ґ Й╦┬Лї─б═(No Sumбґmaбґrizбґing Korea) andб═Koreбґan Newtro.б═Folбґlow him on the social netбґwork forбґmerбґly known as Twitбґter atб═@colinmarshall.

title: When Roald Dahl Wrote a Story Predicting the Rise of ChatGPT and Other AI Large Language Models (1954)
description: Most of us who know the work of Roald Dahl grew up with it, evenбґtuбґalбґly comбґing to conбґsidбґer the man a masбґter of imagбґiбґnaбґtive, often gr
body:
Most of us who know the work of Roald Dahl grew up with it, evenбґtuбґalбґly comбґing to conбґsidбґer the man a masбґter of imagбґiбґnaбґtive, often grotesque tales for chilбґdren. A bit latбґer on, when we heard that heБ─≥d also writбґten books for adults, with titles like Kiss Kiss and Switch Bitch, some of us sought them out as a kind of forбґbidбґden litбґerбґary fruit. What tends to escape notice is that he also wrote for teenagers Б─■ or, in any case, that cerбґtain of his stoбґries were packбґaged for teenagers into the posthuбґmous volбґume The Great Autoбґmatбґic Grammaбґtiбґzaбґtor, whose title stoбґry has gained a new relбґeбґvance in our age of ChatбґGбґPT, as explained in the new Tibees video above.
First pubбґlished in 1954, Б─°The Great Autoбґmatбґic GrammaбґtiбґzaбґtorБ─² conбґcerns an enorбґmousбґly comбґplex, wholбґly anaбґlog machine that can genбґerбґate page after page of text at a then-unimagбґinбґable clip. Its invenбґtor, a beatбґen-down young corбґpoбґrate employбґee called Adolph Knipe, designs it based on the same prinбґciбґples heБ─≥d used to creбґate an elecбґtric calбґcuбґlaбґtor that pleased his boss, Mr. Bohlen. A frusбґtratбґed writer of ficбґtion by night, Knipe conбґceives of the Grammaбґtiбґzaбґtor as a tool of revenge against the magбґaбґzine indusбґtry that spurned him. With the comбґpaбґnyБ─≥s backбґing to build the thing, he tells Bohlen, they could domбґiбґnate the marбґket for short stoбґries almost withбґout effort Б─■ and make their own presбґtiбґgious names as authors to boot.
Б─°It stands to reaбґson that an engine built along the lines of the elecбґtric comбґputбґer could be adjustбґed to arrange words (instead of numбґbers) in their right order accordбґing to the rules of gramбґmar,Б─² Dahl writes. Б─°Give it the verbs, the nouns, the adjecбґtives, the proбґnouns, store them in the memбґoбґry secбґtion as a vocabбґuбґlary, and arrange for them to be extractбґed as required. Then feed it with plots and leave it to write the senбґtences.Б─² Though Bohlen accepts the techбґniбґcal propoбґsiбґtion, he at first doubts the comбґmerбґcial one, at least until his employбґee informs him that magбґaбґzines like the Satбґurбґday Evening Post, CollierБ─≥s, and LadiesБ─≥ Home Jourбґnal will pay for a stoбґry Б─°anyбґthing up to twenбґty-five hunбґdred dolбґlarsБ─²: nearбґly $40,000 today.
Of course, 1954 was a difбґferбґent time. Today, theб═Satбґurбґday Evening Post, CollierБ─≥s, and LadiesБ─≥ Home Jourбґnalб═have all gone, as has the prospect of earnбґing even a meaбґger livбґing through short stoбґries. And a comбґputбґer of this kind, as Dahl describes it, would have been an enorбґmous, noisy device laden with butбґtons, dials, pedбґals, and stops, each of which the Б─°writerБ─² would use to conбґtrol such variбґables as theme, style, tenбґsion, humor, and pasбґsion. Б─°The qualбґiбґty may be infeбґriбґor,Б─² an increasбґingбґly powбґer-mad Knipe admits of the machineБ─≥s outбґput, Б─°but that doesnБ─≥t matбґter. ItБ─≥s the cost of proбґducбґtion that counts.Б─² All of us now posбґsess Grammaбґtiбґzaбґtors of our own, far faster, cheapбґer, more verбґsaбґtile, and easбґiбґer to use than anyбґthing Roald Dahl could have imagбґined. Yet how many of us can hope to be read more than 70 years in the future?
via Metafilбґter
Relatбґed conбґtent:
How George Orwell Preбґdictбґed the Rise of Б─°AI SlopБ─² in Nineбґteen Eighty-Four (1949)
Sci-Fi Writer Arthur C. Clarke Preбґdictбґed the Rise of Artiбґfiбґcial Intelбґliбґgence & the Exisбґtenбґtial Quesбґtions We Would Need to Answer (1978)
Roald Dahl Gives a Tour of the Small Backбґyard Hut Where He Wrote All of His Beloved ChildrenБ─≥s Books
Read a Nevбґer Pubбґlished, Б─°SubбґverбґsiveБ─² Chapбґter from Roald DahlБ─≥s Charбґlie and the Chocoбґlate Facбґtoбґry
Roald Dahl, Who Lost His Daughбґter to Measles, Writes a Heartбґbreakбґing Letбґter about Vacбґciбґnaбґtions: Б─°It Is Almost a Crime to Allow Your Child to Go UnimбґmuбґnisedБ─²
Based in Seoul,б═Colбґinб═Marshallб═writes and broadбґcasts on cities, lanбґguage, and culбґture. HeБ─≥s the author of the newsletбґterб═Books on Citiesб═as well as the booksб═М∙°Й╣ґ Л ■Л∙Ґ Й╦┬Лї─б═(No Sumбґmaбґrizбґing Korea) andб═Koreбґan Newtro.б═Folбґlow him on the social netбґwork forбґmerбґly known as Twitбґter atб═@colinmarshall.

title: David Bowie Picks His 12 Favorite David Bowie Songs
description: Admit it, your list of favorite Bowie songs is full of the big hits. Hell, maybe itБ─≥s all hits; thereБ─≥s no shame in that. Digбґging deep into
body:
Admit it, your list of favorite Bowie songs is full of the big hits. Hell, maybe itБ─≥s all hits; thereБ─≥s no shame in that. Digбґging deep into the crates will yield many an overбґlooked surбґprise, many a subбґtle sleepбґer, cut-up clasбґsic, and elecбґtronбґic experбґiбґment. But if all youБ─≥ve got is ChangesбґbowieБ─■the 1990 comбґpiбґlaбґtion that became, for some genбґerбґaбґtions, a definбґiбґtive stateбґment of his careerБ─■youБ─≥ve still got a colбґlecбґtion of songs the likes of which have nevбґer been heard before or since in modбґern pop.
Comбґpletists may grouch, but even resбґiбґdent Bowie scholars/local record store clerks have an Б─°Ashбґes to Ashбґes,Б─² Б─°Б─≥HeroesБ─≥,Б─² Б─°Changes,Б─² or Б─°Modбґern LoveБ─² in their top ten. Whether ardent or casuбґal fans, we conбґnect with BowieБ─≥s music through mileбґstones, both in his career and in our own lives. This truth has been exploitбґed. In 2008, Mike Schiller at PopбґMatбґters bemoaned the fact that almost 20 Bowie comбґpiбґlaбґtion albums had been released, a few of which Б─°donБ─≥t realбґly seem to court any greater purбґpose whatбґsoбґevбґer.Б─²
Givбґen this surбґfeit of Bowie comбґpiбґlaбґtions on the marбґket, SchillerБ─≥s iniбґtial groanбґing reacбґtion to news of yet anothбґer (Б─°Oh, good Lord. Anothбґer David Bowie colбґlecбґtion?Б─²) seems appoбґsite. Except this colбґlecбґtion, iSEбґLECT: BOWIE, released in 2008 to readбґers of the U.K.Б─≥s Mail on Sunбґday, then latбґer in an offiбґcial CD and digбґiбґtal ediбґtion, Б─°is actuбґalбґly someбґthing speбґcial.Б─² Bowie Б─°picked the trackбґlist himбґself. Even more than that, the trackбґlist actuбґalбґly looksб═like someбґthing heБ─≥d have picked himбґself, rather than havбґing a manбґagбґer or pubбґliбґcist pick it for him.Б─²
iSEбґLECT: BOWIE 
1. Б─°Life On Mars?Б─² (from the album Hunky Dory)
2. Б─°Sweet Thing/Candidate/Sweet ThingБ─² (from the album Diaбґmond Dogs)
3. Б─°The Bewlay BrothбґersБ─² (from the album Hunky Dory)
4. Б─°Lady Grinбґning SoulБ─² (from the album Aladdin Sane)
5. Б─°WinБ─² (from the album Young Amerбґiбґcans)
6. Б─°Some AreБ─² (curбґrentбґly excluбґsive to this comбґpiбґlaбґtion)
7. Б─°Teenage WildlifeБ─² (from the album Scary Monбґsters)
8. Б─°RepбґeбґtiбґtionБ─² (from the album Lodger)
9. Б─°Fanбґtasбґtic VoyбґageБ─² (from the album Lodger)
10. Б─°Lovбґing The AlienБ─² (from the album Tonight)
11. Б─°Time Will Crawl (MM Remix)Б─² (new remix by David Bowie)
12. Б─°Hang On To Yourбґself [live]Б─² (from the album Live Sanбґta Monбґiбґca Б─≥72)
See the full trackбґlist above and hear a playlist of his picks at the top. If we put all our lists of favorites togethбґer, we might see a very high perбґcentбґage of Б─°Life on Mars?Б─² picks. WeБ─≥re in excelбґlent comбґpaбґny; itБ─≥s BowieБ─≥s numбґber one favorite song of his. But how many of his othбґer picks might we choose? The eight-and-a-half minute Б─°Sweet ThingБ─²/Б─²CandidateБ─²/Б─²Sweet Thing (Reprise)Б─² from Diaбґmond Dogs? Б─°WinБ─² from Young Amerбґiбґcansб═or Б─°The Bewlay BrothбґersБ─² from Hunky Dory?
Aside from Б─°Life on Mars?Б─² and the far lessбґer-colбґlectбґed Б─°Lovбґing the AlienБ─² and Б─°Time Will Crawl,Б─² none of his twelve selecбґtions were released as sinбґgles. There are no songs from two of the most acclaimed Bowie albums, Lowб═and Б─≥HeroesБ─≥, unless we count Б─°Some AreБ─² a bonus track includбґed on the Lowб═1991 rereбґlease. There are two tracks fromб═Lodger, the third and least accesбґsiбґble of his vauntбґed Berlin trilбґoбґgy, and only one selecбґtion from Zigбґgy Starбґdust,б═and it ainБ─≥t Б─°Zigбґgy Starбґdust.Б─²
If anyбґone else handбґed you this list of favorite Bowie tracks, youБ─≥d be skepбґtiбґcal. Who puts Б─°Hang On To YourбґselfБ─² (Live Sanбґta Monбґiбґca Б─≥72) above any of the stuбґdio tracks on that clasбґsic 1972 breakбґout album? David Bowie, thatБ─≥s who. And who knows, if youБ─≥d asked him the day before or after, he might have picked twelve difбґferбґent songs. ThereБ─≥s no telling how seriбґousбґly he took the exerбґcise, but in the newsбґpaбґper release, he did Б─°casuбґalбґly [pen] his inspiбґraбґtions for the songs and the recordбґing processбґes behind them,Б─² notes AllmusicБ─≥s Jason Lymanбґgrover.
On his choice of Б─°Teenage Wildlife,Б─² for examбґple, Bowie comбґmentбґed: Б─°So itБ─≥s late mornбґing and IБ─≥m thinkбґing, Б─≤New song and a fresh approach. I know. IБ─≥m going to do a Ronбґnie Specбґtor. Oh yes I am. Ersatz just for one day.Б─≥ And I did and here it is. Bless. IБ─≥m still very enamбґoured of this song and would give you two Б─≤Modбґern LoveБ─≥s for it anyбґtimeБ─іБ─² Bowie got to expeбґriбґence his own music in a way no one else could. iSEбґLECT: BOWIEб═gets behind the greatбґest hits colбґlecбґtions for a glimpse at the way he heard and rememбґbered his catбґaбґlogue.
Note: An earбґliбґer verбґsion of this post appeared on our site in 2019.
Relatбґed Conбґtent:
David BowieБ─≥s 100 Must Read Books
The Art Colбґlecбґtion of David Bowie: An Introбґducбґtion
How David Bowie Used William S. BurбґroughsБ─≥ Cut-Up Method to Write His Unforбґgetбґtable Lyrics
David Bowie Sings Impresбґsions of Bruce Springбґsteen, Lou Reed, Iggy Pop, Tom Waits & More In Stuбґdio Outбґtakes (1985)
Josh Jones is a writer and musiбґcian based in Durham, NC.б═

title: 'Big Walk' Is a New Video Game about ... Walking and Talking
description: In the ever-expanding pantheon of open-world video games where combat, survival, crafting, and anarchy reign, the simple idea of taking a virtual
body:
In the ever-expanding pantheon of open-world video games where combat, survival, crafting, and anarchy reign, the simple idea of taking a virtual walk while chatting with a few friends might seem pointless. A new video game from Melbourne-based developer House House begs to differ, though, turning a casual stroll across dreamy landscapes into a uniquely collaborative game, where puzzles and the lengths required to solve them take center stage. Some areas of Big Walk render players speechless, forcing you to devise innovative ways to communicate. It might just be the antithesis of Fortnite or Grand Theft Auto.
This friendly, casual, and playful approach to game design may come as no surprise from the makers of the critically acclaimed Untitled Goose Game, which is centered entirely on a hapless goose that navigates everyday environments while avoiding the unwanted attention of nearby humans. Б─°As much as Big Walk is a game about walking and talking, itБ─≥s also about exploring, and getting lost, and doing challenges, and sometimes, not really doing anything at all,Б─² shares a game trailer.
You can play this Б─°cooperative online walker-talkerБ─² on Steam, Switch 2, and PlayStation 5 beginning August 4.

schema.headline: 'Big Walk' Is a New Video Game about ... Walking and Talking

title: Delcy Morelos Tends to Sepulchral Installations in a Divine Connection to the Land
description: The Colombian artist Delcy Morelos describes her hometown of Tierralta as Б─°a paradise full of butterflies and unpaved streets.Б─² In the late 1960s
body:
The Colombian artist Delcy Morelos describes her hometown of Tierralta as Б─°a paradise full of butterflies and unpaved streets.Б─² In the late 1960s and early Б─≥70s, Morelos spent her days in her grandmotherБ─≥s garden, running barefoot and gleaning what it meant to live in connection with the land. When paramilitary and guerrilla troops moved in, though, the region was plunged into a chaotic state of grief and fear.
In her earliest works, Morelos translated the death and destruction plaguing her home into two-dimensional compositions. As she details in a new segment for Art21, acrylic painting was not long her primary mode of working, and quickly, she returned to the earth, incorporating soil, straw, and grass into large-scale installations. The film follows the artist as she installs a sepulchral mound in SevilleБ─≥s Centro Andaluz de Arte Contemporц║neo, a former Carthusian monastery turned art museum that once housed Christopher ColumbusБ─≥ remains.



Throughout the film, we witness Morelos grab gobs of straw-laden soil and affix the chunky material to a standing structure, which eventually grows a range of produce native to both sides of the Atlantic. In contrast to the spaceБ─≥s colonial ties, the artistБ─≥s work presents a way of creating and living thatБ─≥s entwined in natural rhythms. Visitors are greeted by notes of cinnamon, cloves, and fecund soil before being enveloped by towering walls of growth. Within the vaulted monastery, MorelosБ─≥ indomitable forms offer a direct tie to the sacred and divine right beneath our feet.
Б─°Many people believe they are in a bubble, and that is why they can do things that harm nature, harm others, and also harm themselves,Б─² she says, demonstrating a profound sense of care for and connection to all that gives life.
This segment is part of Art21Б─≥s Human Nature episode and is available to watch on its site. Find additional films on YouTube.

schema.headline: Delcy Morelos Tends to Sepulchral Installations in a Divine Connection to the Land

title: 'Women in Trees' Celebrates a Quirky Collection of Anonymous Snapshots
description:
All images courtesy of Hatje Cantz, shared with permission
June 11, 2026
BooksHistoryPhotography
Kate Mothes

As collector Jochen Raiц÷ (1969-2022) sc
body:
All images courtesy of Hatje Cantz, shared with permission
June 11, 2026
BooksHistoryPhotography
Kate Mothes

As collector Jochen Raiц÷ (1969-2022) scoured flea markets and antique stalls for the better part of three decades for snapshots, he began to notice a running theme. Over time, he amassed a trove of photos by anonymous photographers with an unusually high number of portraits of women posing in trees. Swiss newspaper Zц╪richer Tagesanzeiger asked, Б─°What are they all doing up there?Б─² And German paper Der Spiegel posited that the arbor-climbing might be a Б─°forgotten popular sport.Б─² Whatever the reason, the mystery is nearly as fun as the photos.
A hardcover edition of Women in Trees from Hatje Cantz, published in German and English, follows two titles published in 2016 and 2017 that celebrate these quirky images. Find your copy on the publisherБ─≥s website.

title: Natural Dyes Merge with Mixed Media in Annalise Neil's Dreamy Cyanotypes
description: Б─°Matter is memory, and memory is a medium,Б─² says artist Annalise Neil, whose surreal cyanotypes brim with animals, fungi, geological specimens, sh
body:
Б─°Matter is memory, and memory is a medium,Б─² says artist Annalise Neil, whose surreal cyanotypes brim with animals, fungi, geological specimens, shells, and more that she augments with watercolor. Recently, the artist has been adding rich, earthy tones with natural dyes such as wild strawberry leaf, oak gall, loquat leaf, and chestnut. She has used botanical teas to shift the natural blue color of the cyanotypes for quite a while, but the sepia tonality has emerged as a larger focus lately, which allows her to layer hues like browns and purples.
NeilБ─≥s experiences in nature profoundly influence her individual pieces in a process that she poetically describes as Б─°melting, rolling, pinching, sanding, walking across meadows, cheek on sun-warmed boulders.Б─²б═This year, sheБ─≥s a resident artist at Volcanб═Mountain Foundation in Julian, California, which merges artistic and scientific inquiry. Б─°I endeavor to create work that will lead to contemplation and reflection and that invites a thoughtful examination of our relationship to reality and our surroundings,Б─² she says. 
Б─°Littoral TalismanБ─² (2026), watercolor and cyanotype toned with madder root, chestnut tannins and strawberry leaf tea on Canson Montval paper, hand-carved wood panel, gouache, volcano keyhole limpet shell, copper, 28 x 18 x 1.75 inches
Б─°For my site-specific work, I begin by hiking for many days and photographing intriguing things I find, including birds andб═mammals, plants, geological forms, and insects,Б─² she says. Б─°As I photograph specimens in wild and cultivated spaces, I capture a brief version of their existence that I transmute into a negative and then into a cyanotype.Б─² The images are then supported on hand-carved wooden panels.
NeilБ─≥s work is currently on view in Fast Forward: Analog Photography as a Third Space at the Los Angeles Center for Photography and Sanguine Glimmers at Hey Books! in San Diego, among others. See more and follow updates on the artistБ─≥s Instagram.
Б─°IntervalsБ─² (2023), watercolor and cyanotype on Awagami Mitsumata paper mounted to acrylic-painted wood panel, 10 x 8 x 1.5 inches
